text: The surgeon mobilizes the distal left ureter, using a hemlock clip attached to a suture to flip and assist in the ureteral mobilization.

Frozen sections of the ureter are sent for intraoperative pathologic analysis. The surgeon mobilizes the posterior prostate through Denonvilliers' fascia, while the right ureter is clipped using a hemlock clip attached to a suture, similar to the technique used on the left ureter.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection towards the area of the right distal ureter dissection. The lateral edge of the bladder is mobilized, separating it from the pelvic sidewall and iliac vessels. The lateral dissection exposes the posterior pedicle, which is divided using a vessel seal device. The obturator nerve is visible, ensuring it remains protected from injury.
The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia on both sides of the prostate, preparing for nerve-sparing dissection in an anti-grade fashion.

The surgeon develops the space of Retzius, followed by apical dissection of the prostate, separating the levator fibers and the dorsal venous complex from the prostate. A dorsal venous fixation stitch is placed to achieve hemostasis and aid incontinence preservation.

The surgeon separates the dorsal venous complex and the recto urethralis muscle from the urethra. The distal urethra is clipped and divided, with frozen sections sent for pathologic analysis. Remaining attachments are taken down in a retroapical fashion, and the specimen is placed in a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag for retrieval.

Pelvic lymphadenectomy as well as intracorporeal ileal loop diversion are performed in the standard fashion as described and published elsewhere. The patient did well postoperatively and was discharged home on postoperative day four. He currently has no evidence of disease recurrence.
